finding: HONESTY NOTE ON THIS PHASE: four blind subagents were spawned (correctness/security/error-handling; patterns/perf/maintainability/modularity; tests-quality/coverage/test-reality; state-management/regression/a11y/responsive). They largely did NOT return structured findings, and one ran a blanket `git checkout --` in the shared worktree and destroyed an uncommitted edit (recovered from context; incident reported by that agent). The findings below are therefore predominantly ones the PARENT verified by RUNNING code, not subagent self-reports. Where a subagent raised a claim it is recorded and marked with how it was adjudicated. This is recorded rather than papered over because a green phase-6 that rests on unread subagent output is exactly the failure mode this gate exists to prevent.

CRLF DEFECT (found + FIXED this round). The trailing-context test was /^[ 	]*(
|$)/, which does not match 
. A CRLF document therefore took the something-follows branch and emitted a stray prefix-plus- line after the closing fence: '- first \[ x_1 \]
- second' produced '...  $$
  
- second'. Reachable via an uploaded .md in the file viewer and a Windows-authored SKILL.md. Fixed with ? in the trailing test, the blank-line runaway guard, and the body split. Pinned by a new CRLF test.

MATH_SPAN_RE matches any two `$` on one line, so prose like 'costs $5 and [docs] is $10' is treated as a math span and skips the reference-link pass. Verified against the real pipeline that this is NOT a regression: singleDollarTextMath is already enabled, so remark-math ALREADY consumes that span as inline math — the pre-change code was injecting '(http://x)' link markup INSIDE the resulting equation. Every differing case is an improvement. Pinned by two assertions in markdownPreprocess.test.ts.

Verified by running the real remark-math -> remark-rehype -> rehype-katex pipeline over 11 cases: both literal issue-#177 equations produce exactly 1 .katex-display each; bullet and ordered lists keep li=2 with the block INSIDE the item; blockquote keeps blockquote=1; a table row keeps td=2 with inline .katex; a fenced code block and an unclosed streaming partial both produce .katex=0. No case produced corrupted surrounding markdown.

ReDoS: timed the new regexes on pathological inputs. 20k unclosed '\[' openers (40KB) = 109ms; 20k unclosed '\(' = 109ms; alternating = 110ms; 200KB single unclosed opener = 0.3ms; 40k bare '$' = 0.0ms; 20k matched pairs (100KB) = 310ms. Linear-to-quadratic, NO catastrophic/exponential backtracking. The O(n^2) worst case is mass UNMATCHED openers, which is not a realistic model emission (a real .tex has matched pairs = the 310ms/100KB case). Recorded as a known bound rather than mitigated.

KaTeX injection: rewriting model text into $$ hands it to KaTeX, so checked the trust surface. Verified by running: \href{javascript:alert(1)}{click} renders with NO anchor emitted (KaTeX `trust` defaults to false, so \href/\url are not honored), and \htmlData is refused with a strict-mode warning ('HTML extension is disabled on strict mode'). No new XSS vector. Note the plugin passes only errorColor, so the safe defaults are in force.

SUBAGENT CLAIM (audit-quality), ADJUDICATED: 'external images are shielded from the blocking pass by a false $...$ span'. The observation is correct at the preprocessMarkdown output level. Re-tested end-to-end across 5 variants ('costs $5 ![alt](https://external.example/pixel.png) and $10' etc.): ZERO elements reach the rendered DOM in any variant, because remark-math consumes the span as math text before an image node can exist. The exfil/tracking-pixel property is intact. Cosmetic output difference only; rejected as a security issue. Counterexample invited from the reporting agent; none supplied.

Checked whether replacing readable plain-text LaTeX with KaTeX output regresses screen readers. Verified by running katex.renderToString: output includes the .katex-mathml branch with a real <math> element and <annotation encoding="application/x-tex"> carrying the original LaTeX, while the visual .katex-html branch is aria-hidden="true". This is an accessibility IMPROVEMENT over reading raw '\frac{d^2C(x)}{dx^2}' character soup.

Runs on every streaming frame, so measured it: a realistic 41KB markdown document (headings, prose, links, inline code, display math, JS fence, GFM table) costs 0.94ms/call for the FULL preprocessMarkdown including the new math pass and nested split. Two indexOf early-outs short-circuit delimiter-free input to ~0. No accidental O(n^2) on realistic input. Acceptable per-frame cost.

Every guard is a return-the-match-unchanged early-out, so no input path can throw or produce partial output. Non-string input returns as-is before any regex runs. continuationPrefix's CONTAINER_RE cannot fail to match (all quantifiers are * or optional) but the null-guard on exec is present anyway. KaTeX render failures are handled upstream by rehype-katex's own try/catch + errorColor, and all 5 call sites remain wrapped in StreamdownErrorBoundary.

The added preprocessMarkdown() call is a pure function evaluated in JSX on each render, not a hook — no Rules-of-Hooks exposure, no reactive store read inside a loop or conditional, no new state. Deliberately NOT memoized: a cache keyed on message id would be a staleness/lifecycle hazard, and at 0.94ms for 41KB it would save nothing measurable. The two module-level `g`-flagged regexes are only used via String.replace/split, which reset lastIndex themselves; no new exec loop was introduced (the pre-existing DEF_RE exec loop still resets lastIndex=0 explicitly).

preprocessMarkdown is shared by all 5 Streamdown call sites after this change. Proved byte-identical output on non-math input by extracting the pre-change implementation with `git show` and running it side-by-side over a 26-input corpus (reference links full/collapsed/shortcut, unresolved refs, footnotes, array indices, external/local/data:/nested images, ``` and ~~~ and inline code, tables, blockquotes, lists, headings, empty, trailing newline): 26/26 identical. The only differing inputs are the $-span cases, adjudicated above as improvements.

Full pre-existing unit suite re-run after every change round and compared against a stashed clean tree: baseline 423 tests / 413 pass / 10 fail; after 456 / 446 / 10 fail — the SAME 10 failures (auth, voice x4, scheduler x2, chat stores x3), all in modules untouched by this diff, all pre-existing. No test regressed.

Mirrors the sibling pre-tokenizer citationTokenize.ts: module-scope named regex consts with an explanatory comment above each, one exported pure function with a JSDoc stating WHY, colocated node:test spec importing the source with an explicit .ts extension and no describe wrapper. Runner choice verified correct — vitest.config.ts is scoped to src/**/*.store.test.ts only, and npm run test:unit drives node:test. Placement in components/common (not modules/chat) matches the fact that 3 of 5 consumers are outside chat, alongside the existing markdownPreprocess.ts.

The two non-obvious parser facts the design depends on (a one-line $$x$$ parses as INLINE math; a line-start $$ DOES interrupt an open paragraph) are documented in the function JSDoc with the reason they matter, because neither is guessable from the code and both were established by running the parser. Guard rationale is inline at each early-out. The only numeric literal is the 4-space indented-code threshold, which is CommonMark-dictated and commented as such.

Adding a future delimiter family (the descoped \begin{align}) requires editing MATH_RE's alternation and adding a branch to the replace callback — it is a closed structure, not an open seam. Accepted deliberately for two delimiter pairs: a registry abstraction here would be more machinery than the thing it abstracts. Flagged so the \begin{env} follow-up refactors rather than bolts on, and because that work additionally needs the splitter taught about existing $$ spans (DEC-6).

Single exported function, string -> string, no options object, no ordering contract for the caller to get wrong. The one non-obvious contract — that it does NOT skip code blocks and relies on its caller's code-fence split — is stated explicitly in the module header rather than left implicit, since calling it standalone on raw markdown would be a latent bug.

Every assertion compares against a literal expected string rather than a regex or a truthiness check, so a test cannot pass vacuously. The idempotence test reuses every input from the other tests via a shared collector rather than a hand-maintained second list, so it cannot silently drift out of coverage. No assertion is tautological (no assert(true), no x===x).

The e2e math assertions use exact counts (.katex-display === 2, .katex === 3) rather than >0 where the exact number is knowable, and additionally assert the raw LaTeX is ABSENT (not.toContainText('\\frac{d^2C(x)}{dx^2}')) — so a regression that rendered nothing at all, or that rendered math while leaking the source, both fail. The code-block spec asserts BOTH .katex===0 AND that the literal delimiters are still visible, so it cannot pass by the block failing to render.

Branch-by-branch map: empty-inner guard (TEST-4), blank-line runaway (TEST-4 + CRLF variant), inline-with-$ skip (TEST-4), body-line-is-$$ guard (TEST-4), table-row downgrade (TEST-4), continuationPrefix null/indented-code branch (TEST-4), prefix reconstruction for bullet/ordered/blockquote (TEST-3), open-branch both ways (TEST-1 block-start, TEST-3 mid-sentence), close-branch both ways (TEST-1 end-of-input, TEST-3 text-follows), CRLF trailing branch (new CRLF test), doubly-escaped opener (TEST-1/2), unmatched opener (TEST-5), idempotence (TEST-5). No unexercised branch remains.

Verified statically that every testid the new specs select actually exists in source: skill-detail-body (SkillDetailDrawer.tsx:248), wf-step-output-accordion- (StepOutputExpander.tsx:103), wf-detail-run-btn (WorkflowDetailDrawer.tsx:163), wf-run-submit-btn (WorkflowRunDialog.tsx:181), wf-progress-status-tag (WorkflowRunProgressView.tsx:128). The skill spec's route-mock matches the real GET /api/skills/{id}/body shape ({body: string}, consumed as res.body at SkillDetailDrawer.tsx:91). TEST-11 deliberately uses the dev-only `mock:` step feature so the markdown is the step's REAL persisted output with NOTHING mocked at the HTTP layer, which is a stronger proof than a route mock.

Reconciled every PLAN ITEM against shipped code: ITEM-1/2/3/4 = normalizeMathDelimiters.ts (MATH_RE, inline branch, continuationPrefix + open/close, five guards); ITEM-5 = markdownPreprocess.ts (import, MATH_SPAN_RE, widened early return, nested j+=2 loop); ITEM-6 = TextContent.tsx:49; ITEM-7 = SkillDetailDrawer.tsx:227,252 + StepOutputExpander.tsx:94; ITEM-8 = markdown-rendering.spec.ts header + inverted test + mermaid comment. ITEM-9 is [DESCOPED] with an approved disposition in DECISIONS.md (DEC-6). No item is covered on paper but absent in code.

A KaTeX display block is wider than prose, so checked containment at every touched call site: chat TextContent wraps in 'w-full overflow-x-auto', the extensions variant the same, file viewer body.tsx likewise, SkillDetailDrawer wraps each Streamdown in 'overflow-auto', StepOutputExpander in 'overflow-auto max-h-80'. A wide equation therefore scrolls inside its own container rather than forcing horizontal PAGE scroll at ~390px. No new container was introduced by this diff.

The math pass is a separate module composed into preprocessMarkdown at ONE call site inside the existing code-split loop, rather than a parallel pipeline or a second entry point — so there is exactly one place where pass ordering is decided and no call site can drift by calling the passes in a different order. STREAMDOWN_PLUGINS and chatMarkdownPlugins are untouched (verified absent from the diff); the fix is entirely upstream of Streamdown and adds no dependency.
